title: activity — domain events
description: Seven events. `ActivityRecorded` is the primary fanout signal — fires on every vendor row insert and drives broadcast to WebSocket + digest queueing + downstream reactions. `ActivityBatchStarted / Completed` bracket a single HTTP request so collapsed feed rendering can group same-batch activities. `ActivityRetentionPolicyCreated / Updated` are admin CRUD signals. `ActivityPurgeExecuted` is retention plumbing. `ActivityFeedSubscribed` captures reads for feed-engagement analytics (functional consent tier). All events implement `ShouldDispatchAfterCommit` — activity rows must be durably persisted before downstream reactions fire.

events.name: ActivityRecorded
events.trigger: ActivityObserver@created fires after the vendor row is persisted + tenant_id / application_id are stamped. The primary broadcast + reaction signal — every downstream consumer that wants to react to activity subscribes here.
consumers: notifications::QueueActivityDigestForRecipients (opt-in per user — collect for daily/weekly digest email)

events.name: ActivityRetentionPolicyCreated
events.trigger: POST /api/v1/activity-retention-policies completed successfully — a new retention override just landed.
consumers: compliance::EmitRetentionOverrideCreatedSignal (SOC 2 CC6.3 evidence — every retention decision is compliance-worthy)

events.name: ActivityRetentionPolicyUpdated
events.trigger: PATCH /api/v1/activity-retention-policies/{policy} completed — retention_days or override_reason or expires_at changed. Fires only when the diff is non-trivial (skipping when only updated_at / updated_by moved).
consumers: notifications::MaybeDispatchRegulatorHoldNotification (fires when the threshold is crossed either way)

events.name: ActivityBatchStarted
events.trigger: ActivityBatcher.beginBatch(requestId) at the start of an HTTP request that WILL generate multiple related activities. Dispatched by the `activity.enforce_scope` middleware on tenant routes.
events.note: NOT afterCommit — the batch start signals the beginning of a transactional group. Rolled-back requests emit the ActivityBatchStarted but no ActivityRecorded rows for that batch — this is fine (empty batch); ReconcileActivityBatchesJob sweeps empty batches on schedule.

events.name: ActivityBatchCompleted
events.trigger: ActivityBatcher.completeBatch(requestId) on request terminate. Fires when the request wrote at least one activity — empty batches emit no completion event.
consumers: activity::EmitBatchCollapsedFeedItem (renders 'Alice made N changes' as one collapsed row on the feed WebSocket channel)

events.name: ActivityPurgeExecuted
events.trigger: PurgeExpiredActivitiesJob or ArchiveActivitiesToColdStorageJob finished a sweep. Fires once per job run (not per row deleted / archived) so downstream metrics can aggregate correctly.

events.name: ActivityFeedSubscribed
events.trigger: A user opened the activity feed dashboard OR subscribed to a specific tenant.{tenantId}.activity WebSocket channel. Dispatched by the /api/v1/activities/feed endpoint on successful subscription auth. Sampled at 10% (feed opens are frequent — full-rate capture would dominate analytics volume).

payload.logNamesFiltered: array<string> (which log_names the caller is subscribing to)

consumers: growth::analytics::CaptureFeedEngagementFunnel (functional consent tier — how often do users check the feed?)

notes: ActivityRecorded is the workhorse — every downstream reaction (broadcast, digest queue, analytics, monitoring) subscribes to this one event. The others are auxiliary.
ActivityRetentionPolicyCreated / Updated are the two events compliance cares about most — every retention decision on activity data is regulator-relevant. See compliance.json for the evidence trail.

ActivityBatchStarted fires SYNCHRONOUSLY (not afterCommit) because it signals request scope, not persistence. A rolled-back request emits the start but no row — the batch is empty and gets swept by ReconcileActivityBatchesJob on schedule. This is intentional and cheap.
ActivityPurgeExecuted fires once per job run, not per row — otherwise a large purge would fan out millions of events.
